annotations.label: Hausdorff dimension (roughness/fractal dimension)

anchor.snippet: the Hausdorff dimension is a measure of roughness

annotations.note: `dimH s` is the Hausdorff dimension of a set in an (e)metric space, the article's fractal-dimension notion.

annotations.label: Dimension of point, segment, square, cube

anchor.snippet: the Hausdorff dimension of a single point is zero

annotations.note: Point (`dimH_singleton` = 0), segment (`Real.dimH_segment` = 1), and filled square/cube (`Real.dimH_of_nonempty_interior` = finrank) are all special cases formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Hausdorff dimension associated with a metric space

anchor.snippet: a dimensional number associated with a metric space

annotations.note: `dimH` is defined for any `[EMetricSpace X]`, exactly the metric-space-associated dimension.

annotations.label: Hausdorff dimension of n-dimensional inner product space

anchor.snippet: the Hausdorff dimension of an n -dimensional inner product space equals n

annotations.note: `Real.dimH_univ_eq_finrank` proves `dimH univ = finrank ℝ E` for any finite-dimensional real normed space, of which an n-dimensional inner product space is a special case.

annotations.label: Koch snowflake

anchor.snippet: the Koch snowflake shown at right is constructed from an equilateral triangle

annotations.note: The Koch snowflake set and its dimension log4/log3 are not constructed in Mathlib (only `Metric.Snowflaking` references it in prose).

annotations.label: Topological (Lebesgue covering) dimension

anchor.snippet: the greatest integer n such that in every covering of X by small open balls

annotations.note: Mathlib formalizes the small inductive dimension, a related topological dimension, but not the Lebesgue covering dimension specifically.

annotations.label: Hausdorff dimension via covering by balls

anchor.snippet: the Hausdorff dimension is the unique number d such that

annotations.note: The characterizing property (μH[d] jumps between 0 and ∞ at d = dimH) is captured by `dimH_def`, `hausdorffMeasure_of_lt_dimH`, and `hausdorffMeasure_of_dimH_lt`, with Mathlib using diameter coverings that yield the same dimension.

annotations.label: Box-counting dimension

anchor.snippet: this defines the box-counting dimension

annotations.note: The box-counting (Minkowski) dimension is not defined in Mathlib.

annotations.label: d-dimensional Hausdorff measure

anchor.snippet: defining first the d-dimensional Hausdorff measure

annotations.note: `MeasureTheory.Measure.hausdorffMeasure d` (notation `μH[d]`) is the d-dimensional Hausdorff measure, with `hausdorffMeasure_apply` giving the covering formula.

annotations.label: Hausdorff dimension (formal)

anchor.snippet: The Hausdorff dimension

annotations.note: `dimH` is the formal Hausdorff dimension defined via the Hausdorff measure.

annotations.label: Hausdorff dimension as supremum characterization

anchor.snippet: This is the same as the supremum of the set

annotations.note: `dimH_def` states `dimH s = ⨆ (d : ℝ≥0) (_ : μH[d] s = ∞), d`, exactly the supremum characterization.

annotations.label: Unlimited Hausdorff content

anchor.snippet: unlimited Hausdorff content of

annotations.note: No Hausdorff content (covering without diameter restriction) is defined in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Countable sets have dimension 0

anchor.snippet: Countable sets have Hausdorff dimension

annotations.note: `dimH_countable` (aliased `Set.Countable.dimH_zero`) proves countable sets have Hausdorff dimension 0.

annotations.label: Euclidean space and circle

anchor.snippet: The Euclidean space

annotations.note: `Real.dimH_univ_pi_fin` proves `dimH (univ : Set (Fin n → ℝ)) = n`; the circle's dimension 1 is not separately formalized.

annotations.label: Cantor set dimension

anchor.snippet: the Cantor set , a zero-dimensional topological space

annotations.note: The ternary `cantorSet` is defined but its Hausdorff dimension log2/log3 is not computed in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Sierpinski triangle dimension

anchor.snippet: The Sierpinski triangle is a union of three copies of itself

annotations.note: The Sierpinski triangle and its dimension log3/log2 are not formalized (Mathlib's `Sierpinski` refers to the unrelated Sierpiński topological space).

annotations.label: Space-filling curves

anchor.snippet: Space-filling curves like the Peano curve

annotations.note: Space-filling curves like the Peano curve and their dimension are not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Brownian motion trajectories

anchor.snippet: Trajectories of Brownian motion through space

annotations.note: Brownian motion is not present in Mathlib, let alone the dimension of its trajectories.

annotations.label: Coastline dimensions (Richardson)

anchor.snippet: the approximate Hausdorff dimension for various coastlines

annotations.note: Empirical coastline dimensions are not a mathematical statement and are not in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Hausdorff dimension vs inductive dimension

anchor.snippet: Suppose X is non-empty

annotations.note: Mathlib has `smallInductiveDimension` but no theorem comparing it with Hausdorff dimension `dimH`.

annotations.label: Hausdorff vs Minkowski dimension

anchor.snippet: The Minkowski dimension is similar to, and at least as large as

annotations.note: The Minkowski dimension is not defined in Mathlib, so the comparison cannot be stated.

annotations.label: Mass distribution / Frostman bound

anchor.snippet: If there is a measure μ defined on Borel subsets of a metric space X

annotations.note: The mass distribution principle / Frostman bound is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Hausdorff dimension of countable unions

anchor.snippet: is a finite or countable union

annotations.note: `dimH_iUnion` (with `dimH_bUnion`, `dimH_sUnion`, `dimH_union`) proves the dimension of a countable union is the supremum of the dimensions.

annotations.label: Hausdorff dimension of products

anchor.snippet: the Hausdorff dimension of their product satisfies

annotations.note: No lemma bounding `dimH (s ×ˢ t)` by `dimH s + dimH t` exists in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Self-similar set

anchor.snippet: a set E is self-similar if it is the fixed point of a set-valued transformation

annotations.note: Self-similar sets / iterated function systems are not defined in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Existence of unique invariant compact set

anchor.snippet: are each a contraction mapping on R n with contraction constant

annotations.note: The ingredients (Banach fixed point `ContractingWith.exists_fixedPoint` and completeness of `NonemptyCompacts` under Hausdorff distance) exist, but Hutchinson's attractor theorem for an IFS is not assembled in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Open set condition

anchor.snippet: There is an open set V with compact closure

annotations.note: The open set condition for iterated function systems is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Dimension of self-similar set under OSC

anchor.snippet: Suppose the open set condition holds and each ψ i is a similitude

annotations.note: The Moran–Hutchinson formula for the dimension of a self-similar set under the open set condition is not formalized.

annotations.label: Self-similarity intersection criterion

anchor.snippet: is self-similar if and only if the intersections satisfy the following condition

annotations.note: This self-similarity intersection criterion is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Fixed point is self-similar

anchor.snippet: the unique fixed point of ψ is self-similar

annotations.note: Self-similarity of the IFS fixed point is not formalized since self-similar sets are undefined in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Moran equation

anchor.snippet: can be determined using the Moran equation

annotations.note: The Moran equation determining the similarity dimension is not formalized in Mathlib.
